topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the faded ink on the parchment, his breath catching in his throat. The biographical sketch, a document he’d found tucked inside a first-edition novel he’d bought at a dusty antique shop, detailed his life with an unnerving precision. Every triumph, every quiet sorrow, every pivotal decision—it was all there, meticulously cataloged, yet none of it was his own voice. He reread the passage describing his early struggles, the descriptions so intimate they felt like an intrusion. A slow, trembling hand rose to cover his mouth, muffling a sharp intake of air. He looked around the quiet study, the familiar bookshelves suddenly seeming like silent witnesses to a secret vastness he had just uncovered. The world outside the window, the mundane sounds of the city, faded entirely. He felt a strange lightness, as if a heavy weight had been lifted, replaced by a dizzying sense of profound recognition mixed with utter disorientation.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," Sarah whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the cavernous library. She held the printed biography, the paper cool against her fingertips. The narrative flowed seamlessly, capturing the nuances of her childhood fears and her unexpected career pivot, all rendered in prose that felt startlingly familiar. Her eyes scanned the text, pausing on a sentence that described an internal conflict she hadn't even consciously acknowledged. A flush crept up her neck, warming her skin. She leaned back against the worn velvet of the armchair, her posture rigid, her gaze fixed on the words as if they held some hidden key. A small, involuntary shudder ran through her frame. She closed her eyes briefly, then opened them, her expression one of stunned, almost reverent stillness. The air in the room seemed thick, charged with the weight of this revelation.

[story 3]

He sat at the kitchen table, the fluorescent light humming overhead, illuminating the stark white pages of the document. When he finished reading the final chapter—a summary of his most defining moments—he didn't move for a long moment. His fingers curled into a tight fist on the edge of the table, the knuckles turning white. A low, almost inaudible hum vibrated in his chest. He slowly lowered the paper, his eyes wide, taking in the sheer scope of the narrative. It wasn't just a record; it was an interpretation, a curated reality. A slow smile spread across his face, a strange, breathless expression that made his cheeks feel hot. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of the wood against the tile floor sounding deafeningly loud in the silence. He stood up, walking toward the window, his movements uncoordinated, as if gravity had momentarily forgotten him.
